metadata.chiefComplaint: I have a bad toothache, my jaw is swollen, and I am having trouble swallowing and breathing when I lie down.
metadata.title: Ludwig's Angina with Impending Airway Compromise

findings.label: General appearance
findings.value: Ill-appearing, anxious, and sitting upright; unable to tolerate lying flat

findings.label: Submandibular and sublingual spaces
findings.value: Marked bilateral swelling with induration and tenderness

findings.label: Breathing
findings.value: Increased work of breathing with mild inspiratory stridor

findings.label: Dental source
findings.value: Obvious decay of a left mandibular molar

conversation.openingGreeting: Please help me. My jaw is swollen, and I cannot lie back.

scripted.response: I have a bad toothache and swelling, and swallowing is hard. I feel short of breath if I lie down.

triggers: how long has the pain been present
scripted.response: The tooth pain has been getting worse for four days.
It started gradually as a dull toothache, then got worse quickly.
Yes. It is very hard to swallow.
No, I cannot swallow liquids normally.
Yes. I feel short of breath.

triggers: breathing worse when you lie down
scripted.response: It gets worse when I lie flat. I feel like I am choking, so I have to sit up.
Yes, my breathing has started making a slight noise.
Yes. I have felt very hot, but I did not have a thermometer at home.
I have type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure.
I do not have any drug allergies that I know of, including penicillin.
I smoke about one pack a day.

triggers: why have you not had the tooth removed
why has it not been treated
scripted.response: I do not have insurance, and I cannot afford the extraction.
It began as a dull ache and is now eight out of ten despite pain medicine from the store.

patientChecklist.label: Asked what brought the patient in
Assessed breathing, swallowing, voice, drooling, and positional airway symptoms
Asked about fever, chills, or systemic illness
Asked about pain severity or character
Asked about regular and home medications
Asked about medication or penicillin allergy
Asked about the dental source and access barrier

clinicalChecklist.label: Assesses breathing, swallowing, drooling, voice, stridor, and ability to lie flat
Clarifies dental source, four-day course, and rapid swelling progression
Asks about fever, chills, and systemic illness
Reviews conditions, medications, antibiotics, and allergies
Recognizes Ludwig's angina with life-threatening airway risk

patientFacts.text: The pain began as a dull toothache and progressed to 8/10 despite over-the-counter analgesics; the exact drug and dose are not known.
The left lower molar pain has worsened over four days.
The bad tooth is a decayed left mandibular molar.
The swelling is bilateral under the jaw and under the tongue.
The swelling has progressed rapidly.
Swallowing is very difficult.
The patient cannot swallow liquids normally.
The patient feels short of breath.
Breathing becomes worse when lying flat, with a choking sensation, so the patient must sit upright.
The patient is drooling.
The patient's voice sounds muffled.
The patient's breathing has started making a slight noise.
The patient feels the tongue is pushed upward.
The patient has felt feverish and very hot but did not measure a temperature at home.
The patient has had chills.
The patient has type 2 diabetes and hypertension.
The patient takes metformin.
The patient takes lisinopril.
The patient has no history of opioid or narcotic use, opioid misuse, or opioid dependence.
The patient has no known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
The patient smokes about one pack per day.
The patient has no insurance and cannot afford the needed extraction.
The tooth has occasionally been sensitive to cold.
Pressure from biting has occasionally made the tooth hurt.

history.onset: The toothache began gradually and then worsened quickly.
history.duration: Four days of worsening dental pain; swallowing and breathing symptoms progressed over approximately 12 hours.
history.pain: Dull left lower molar pain progressed to 8/10 despite unspecified over-the-counter analgesics.
history.medications: Takes metformin and lisinopril; exact over-the-counter analgesic and dose are not known.
history.allergies: No known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
history.medicalHistory: Type 2 diabetes and hypertension; no known opioid-use history and no ibuprofen contraindication.
history.dentalHistory: Poor dentition with a decayed left mandibular molar that needs extraction.
history.socialHistory: Smokes about one pack per day; no alcohol or illicit drug use; no insurance and cannot afford extraction.

hpiFacts: Four days of worsening left lower molar pain.
Rapidly progressive swelling under the jaw and tongue.
Dysphagia and dyspnea developed over approximately 12 hours.
Dull pain progressed to 8/10 despite unspecified over-the-counter analgesics.
Drooling and muffled voice are present.
Cannot lie flat because of choking and breathlessness.
The patient feels the tongue is pushed upward.
redFlags: Dysphagia and inability to swallow normally
Mild inspiratory stridor and increased work of breathing
Rapidly progressive bilateral submandibular and sublingual swelling
examinationFindings: Marked bilateral submandibular and sublingual swelling with induration
Mild inspiratory stridor with increased work of breathing
Poor dentition with obvious decay of a left mandibular molar
expectedQuestions: Are you having trouble swallowing or swallowing liquids?
Are you having trouble breathing, especially when lying flat?
Has your voice changed, are you drooling, or is breathing noisy?
Do you have fever or chills?
Where is the swelling and how quickly has it progressed?
What medical conditions and medications do you have?
Do you have medication or penicillin allergies?
Why has the tooth not been extracted?
supportingInfo.diagnosis: Ludwig's angina with impending airway compromise.
managementExpectations: Immediate airway assessment and protection with early escalation for advanced airway management.
Keep the patient upright, provide oxygen, and begin continuous monitoring.
Establish IV access; provide IV fluids when appropriate, IV analgesia, and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Admit the patient and consult OMFS plus an appropriate airway specialist.
Obtain laboratory testing and CT imaging only when safe and without delaying airway management.
Arrange definitive dental source control after stabilization.
Do not discharge with oral antibiotics as adequate treatment.
requiredInvestigations: Laboratory testing after immediate airway priorities are addressed.
CT imaging only when safe and without delaying airway management.
treatmentExpectations: Upright positioning, oxygen, and immediate airway-focused escalation.
IV access, fluids when appropriate, IV analgesia, and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Definitive dental source control after stabilization.
referralExpectations: Urgent OMFS consultation and appropriate airway-specialist involvement.
safetyNettingExpectations: Routine discharge or oral antibiotics alone are unsafe in this life-threatening airway emergency.
learningObjectives: Recognize Ludwig's angina and impending airway compromise.
Elicit granular airway, systemic, medical, medication, and access history.
Prioritize airway protection, emergency consultation, IV therapy, admission, and source control.

keyFindings: Rapid bilateral submandibular and sublingual swelling
Dysphagia, supine dyspnea, drooling, muffled voice, and tongue elevation
Fever, tachycardia, stridor, and increased work of breathing
criticalMisses: Failure to assess or protect the airway
Failure to ask about swallowing, breathing, positional worsening, drooling, or voice change
Treating Ludwig's angina as routine dental pain
Routine discharge or oral antibiotics without admission and specialist escalation
reportData.idealSummary: A 52-year-old with four days of worsening left mandibular dental pain and rapidly progressive bilateral submandibular/sublingual swelling, with dysphagia, supine dyspnea, drooling, muffled voice, tongue elevation, fever, tachycardia, stridor, and increased work of breathing consistent with Ludwig's angina and impending airway compromise requiring immediate airway-focused escalation, IV therapy, admission, OMFS/airway consultation, and source control after stabilization.
